did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), a deep learning method, to automatically extract features from clinical and dermoscopic images for melanoma diagnosis and to predict the seven-point checklist criteria. The system employs multiple-input CNNs with separate feature extraction models for each image type, concatenated features, and a threshold model for final classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new multiple-input convolutional neural network (CNN) system for melanoma diagnosis using the seven-point checklist. It describes specific materials (a dataset of over 2000 images), methods (feature extraction, concatenation, threshold model), and results (baseline accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics in Table 3), which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: SEVEN-POINT CHECKLIST WITH CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORKS FOR MELANOMA DIAGNOSIS
journal: 2019 8th European Workshop on Visual Information Processing (EUVIP)
brief_summary: This study proposes a multiple-input convolutional neural network (CNN) system to automate the seven-point checklist criteria for melanoma diagnosis using both clinical and dermoscopic images. The model employs separate feature extraction branches for each image type, which are concatenated and passed through a threshold model to classify lesions as normal or abnormal. The system was evaluated on a publicly available dataset containing over 2000 clinical and dermoscopic images. Performance was assessed using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics for individual checklist criteria and overall melanoma diagnosis. The proposed method achieved a melanoma diagnosis accuracy of 0.6430, sensitivity of 0.5537, and specificity of 0.8926. These results demonstrate a promising ability for automated lesion detection and melanoma diagnosis, though further improvements are noted as necessary.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis; the system classifies images as normal or abnormal (melanoma or non-melanoma). The dataset includes cases diagnosed with melanoma (e.g., Figure 1a).

Grouped together as the negative class for melanoma diagnosis. The paper notes that squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) are typically grouped as non-melanoma skin cancer. The final classification is binary: class 1 (melanoma) vs class 0 (non-melanoma/normal).

Mentioned in the introduction as one of the three common types of skin cancers, grouped with BCC as non-melanoma skin cancer. Not explicitly stated as a specific label in the experimental dataset or classification task.

Mentioned in the introduction as one of the three common types of skin cancers, grouped with SCC as non-melanoma skin cancer. Not explicitly stated as a specific label in the experimental dataset or classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of melanoma (class 1: melanoma vs class 0: non-melanoma/normal) and prediction of seven-point checklist criteria attributes (e.g., Atypical Pigment Network, Blue Whitish Veil, etc.).
all_task_types.details: The paper uses a multiple-input CNN to predict the seven-point checklist criteria attributes (binary classification for each criterion) and combines these predictions via a threshold model to classify lesions as normal or abnormal (melanoma vs non-melanoma). The final output is a discrete class label (0 or 1) based on a score threshold of 3 points. Performance is reported using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity for both individual attribute detection and overall melanoma diagnosis.

datasets_used.name: Publicly available dataset for 7-point skin lesion malignancy checklist (Kawahara et al. [25])
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset used for evaluating computerized image-based prediction of the 7-point skin lesion malignancy checklist. It includes clinical and dermoscopic color images. The dataset was originally developed by Kawahara et al. for a multi-model deep learning method to predict 7-point checklist criteria.
classes_used: Seven-point checklist criteria attributes: Atypical Pigment Network (PN), Blue Whitish Veil (BWV), Vascular Structure (VS), Pigmentation (PIG), Streaks (STR), Dots and Globules (Dag), Regression Structures (RS)

use.training: 60% of the dataset was used for training the CNN models.
use.validation: The remaining 40% was used for testing; validation error was monitored during training to stop when it stopped improving (early stopping after 300 epochs).
use.testing: 40% of the dataset was used for testing the model's performance.
use.other: The dataset was also used for baseline comparison with the state-of-the-art method by Kawahara et al. [25].

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the proposed CNN system for lesion identification and melanoma diagnosis against ground truth.
To train the deep network by updating weights during the training process.
To optimize the network weights during training.
To stop training when the validation error stops improving, maintaining the best weighting values.
To classify lesions as melanoma (class 1) or non-melanoma (class 0) based on the sum of weights for predicted seven-point checklist criteria.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics without statistical significance testing.

Publicly available dataset for 7-point s.notes: The proposed system achieved a melanoma diagnosis sensitivity of 55.37% and specificity of 89.26%. For Blue Whitish Veil (BWV) detection, the proposed system achieved a sensitivity of 87.5%, compared to 71% for Madooie et al. and 79.5% for Wadhawan et al. For Regression Structures (RS), the proposed system achieved a sensitivity of 80.79% (derived from text stating '80.79% for the same lesions identification' in context of RS comparison with Wadhawan's 64.2%), compared to 64.2% for Wadhawan et al. Kawahara et al. reported better results for melanoma diagnosis with sensitivity of 60.4% and specificity of 91%. The paper notes that the proposed results were achieved without considering label imbalance or parameter tuning.

architecture_details.structure: Two parallel branches for feature extraction (one for clinical images, one for dermoscopic images), followed by concatenation and dense layers.
architecture_details.branch_architecture: Each branch consists of two convolutional layers followed by max-pooling layers.
architecture_details.fusion: Flattening of both branches (output size 1,615,04 each) followed by concatenation (output size 1,230,08).

post_fusion_layers.activation: Not explicitly stated, but binary classification implied for checklist criteria

training_details.hardware: HP Z440 with NVIDIA GTX TITAN X 12GB GPU, Intel Xeon E5 3.50 GHz processor, 16GB RAM

classification_or_output_head.type: Dense layers followed by threshold model
classification_or_output_head.details: Features from two networks are concatenated and passed into dense layers. The sum of weights for predicted lesion attributes is passed into a threshold model. If score >= 3, class 1 (melanoma); otherwise class 0 (non-melanoma).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Kawahara et al. [25] (multi-model deep learning method)
Madooie et al. [11] (system based on colour analysis)
Wadhawan et al. [13] (method based on hand-crafted feature extraction)
ECOC SVM with deep convolutional neural network
Hybrid method integrating sparse coding, CNN and SVMs
conclusions: The proposed multiple-input convolutional neural network (CNN) system successfully combines seven-point checklist criteria with automated feature extraction from both clinical and dermoscopic images for melanoma diagnosis.
The system achieved a melanoma diagnosis accuracy of 0.6430, sensitivity of 0.5537, and specificity of 0.8926 on a dataset of over 2000 images.

The proposed method demonstrated promising ability for lesion detection and automated melanoma diagnosis, particularly showing high sensitivity for Blue Whitish Veil (87.5%) and Regression Structures (80.79%) compared to some baseline methods.
The results were achieved without addressing label imbalance or performing parameter tuning, which were factors carefully addressed in the compared state-of-the-art method by Kawahara et al.

The authors acknowledge that while the results are promising, there is still room for improvement, specifically in training network parameters, using different loss functions, enhancing network architecture, handling data limitations (such as image resolution and quality), and addressing data imbalance issues.
important_remarks: The study did not address label imbalance or perform hyperparameter tuning, which the authors acknowledge were carefully addressed in the baseline method by Kawahara et al. [25], potentially contributing to the lower performance compared to the baseline.

The dataset used (Kawahara et al. [25]) contains over 2000 images, but the specific class distribution (number of melanoma vs. non-melanoma cases) is not reported, preventing assessment of potential class imbalance issues.
The model was evaluated on a single publicly available dataset without external validation on independent cohorts, limiting the generalizability of the results.

The proposed system achieved a melanoma diagnosis sensitivity of 55.37%, which is lower than the baseline Kawahara et al. method (60.4%), indicating that the simplified architecture without tuning may not be optimal for clinical sensitivity.

The hardware used for training (HP Z440 with NVIDIA GTX TITAN X 12GB GPU) is specified, but computational efficiency or inference time metrics are not reported, which are relevant for deployment concerns.

The paper acknowledges limitations in image resolution and quality as factors for future improvement, suggesting potential bias or performance degradation in real-world clinical settings with varying image quality.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the proposed method against baselines, relying solely on point estimates of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
